topic: Alloc - Rc<T> and Arc<T> Internals and Weak References

turns.content: I'm a frontend dev who just heard about this new reference counting thing in Rust called Rc. Is it basically like how we handle state updates in React?

Not quite, because Rc<T> actually uses a single allocation that holds both the strong count and weak references inside one structure rather than separate boxes. The memory layout packs the reference count and the data together so you don't get double indirection like in React's state updates.
Oh I see, so when we clone a value it just bumps that counter without copying the whole thing?

Exactly, cloning an Rc<T> increments the reference count atomically and reuses the same heap allocation. If you need mutable access though you'd use Arc::make_mut to do clone-on-write which is different from just bumping a counter.
Wait so if I have an Rc and call downgrade does it actually create a Weak<T>?

Yes, Rc::downgrade returns a Weak<T> that shares the same underlying allocation as the original Rc. If you try to dereference a Weak when no strong references remain it fails safely instead of dangling.
That's interesting but what about Arc? Is it just like Rc but better?

Arc<T> is similar to Rc but uses atomic operations for the reference count so it's thread-safe, whereas Rc requires a mutex if you need shared mutable access across threads. You can also use Arc::clone instead of Rc::clone when passing ownership in multithreaded contexts.
So Weak<T> is only useful for breaking cycles right?

Correct, Rc::downgrade helps break reference cycles because a cycle of strong references alone would leak memory. By holding at least one weak link in the chain you can detect when everything has been dropped without needing extra bookkeeping.
What about ptr_eq? Can I use that to check if two Rcs point to the same memory?

Yes, you can use ptr_eq on the internal pointers of Rc or Arc instances to verify identity without dereferencing. This is useful when implementing caching or checking if two references are really the same object before doing expensive work.

topic: Alloc - Rc<T> and Arc<T> Internals and Weak References

turns.content: I'm still stuck on why my CI fails when I swap a single-threaded heap type for something with atomic reference counting. Can you explain the exact memory layout difference between Rc<T> and Arc<T> so I know what to optimize?

The core distinction is that Rc<T> allocates a single block containing both the data pointer and an atomic reference count, while Arc<T> adds another layer of indirection because it must be thread-safe. In memory terms, Rc uses one allocation with a strong counter embedded alongside your value's pointer, whereas Arc typically requires two allocations: one for the shared control block managing the count and another for the actual data. This dual-allocation pattern in Arc prevents unsynchronized access to the reference count from multiple threads without using Mutex or similar synchronization primitives.
That sounds expensive, isn't it? I thought Rc was enough for most cases and only needed Arc when threading. Why would I ever pay this extra cost if the reference count is just one?

You're right that Rc is sufficient in single-threaded contexts, but remember that Rust's compiler cannot guarantee thread safety for you unless you explicitly use types like Arc. If your code runs on multiple threads and uses Rc directly without wrapping it in something like Arc or using a Mutex around the count, the runtime will fail with a data race error during compilation even if your logic seems correct. The extra allocation in Arc isn't just overhead; it's the price we pay for the compiler to verify that no two threads are modifying the reference count simultaneously.
Okay, but what about cycles? I've seen people mention Weak references breaking them. How does that work if Rc only has a strong counter?

That's exactly why Rust provides Weak<T> alongside Rc. While Rc holds a strong reference that increments the count, Weak<T> stores the same underlying data pointer but doesn't touch the atomic counter itself. When you have a cycle like A holding B and B holding A, both nodes keep each other alive through their strong references. By introducing a weak reference to one side of the cycle, you allow garbage collection to reclaim that node once all strong pointers are dropped, effectively breaking the circular dependency without needing manual cleanup logic.
I see now how Weak helps with cycles. But there's still this thing about Rc::try_unwrap and Arc::make_mut that I haven't grasped yet. When exactly do these come into play?

Rc::try_unwrap is useful when you know the reference count has dropped to one, allowing safe extraction of T without cloning. This happens in scenarios where you're transitioning from a shared ownership model to exclusive ownership and want to avoid unnecessary duplication. Similarly, Arc::make_mut comes into play when you need to modify data inside an Arc-wrapped value; instead of copying or taking ownership first, it clones the control block atomically so you can mutate the original without creating a new allocation.

topic: Alloc - Rc<T> and Arc<T> Internals and Weak References

turns.content: What's the difference between using an Rc reference count layout with a weak pointer inside versus just wrapping everything in an Arc atomic reference counting?

The main difference is how they handle ownership cycles and memory safety. An Rc<T> combined with a Weak<T> lets you store both a strong owner and potential observers in the same allocation, so if you need to break a cycle or detect when something becomes unreachable, the weak part tells you immediately without scanning other references. Arc<T> uses atomic reference counting which is faster for simple clone-on-write scenarios but doesn't support seeing when the last owner dies because it only counts strong pointers. You can use Rc::downgrade to get that weak handle safely, and then check if ptr_eq between your current pointer and the weak one to see if the object still exists before dereferencing.

So I was trying to build a shared graph structure where nodes might reference each other, but my code panics when I try to remove a node because I'm holding only strong pointers. How do I safely extract the actual value without worrying about double-free?

You should use Rc::try_unwrap on your single owner before removing it from the graph, which will panic if there are other strong references and return false otherwise so you don't crash unexpectedly. For the graph itself, store each node with its children as an Arc<T> for clone-on-write when sharing data, but keep a Weak<T> in every edge to avoid cycles that would prevent garbage collection or cause panics on removal. When checking if a node still exists before dereferencing its edges, use ptr_eq to compare the address of your strong pointer against each weak reference without allocating extra memory.
Ah okay so I can mix these patterns now? Like using Arc for clone-on-write data sharing and Rc with Weak inside for cycle-safe graph storage?

Exactly that's a common pattern. You can use Arc::make_mut to safely mutate shared data without cloning, and combine it with Rc::downgrade to create a cycle-safe graph where removing nodes won't panic because the weak edges will become invalid. Just remember to handle the borrow checker carefully when you need both mutable access via make_mut and simultaneous observation via weak references in the same scope.
